---
title: "Ch 8 — Algebraic Expressions and Identities"
subtitle: "Multiply rectangles by their parts — the four identities of school algebra"
lesson-id: "cbse8.algebraic-expressions"
tier: "cbse8"
ncert-class: 8
ncert-chapter: 8
ncert-chapter-title: "Algebraic Expressions and Identities"
board-exam: false
prerequisites: ["t1.intro-variables", "t1.multiply-as-area"]
unlocks: ["cbse8.factorisation", "cbse9.polynomials", "cbse10.polynomials"]
applied-domains: ["research", "ai-ml"]
estimated-minutes: 35
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

Be honest before you begin.

1. Can you read $3x^2 - 2x + 5$ as "three times an unknown squared,
   minus two times the unknown, plus five"? If not, revisit
   **[Intro to Variables](../../tier-1-foundations/11-intro-variables/index.qmd)**.
2. Can you compute $(7 + 3) \times 4$ and $7 \times 4 + 3 \times 4$ and
   confirm they're equal? If not, revisit
   **[Multiplication as Area](../../tier-1-foundations/05-multiply-as-area/index.qmd)**.
3. Are you comfortable with $a + a = 2a$ and $a \cdot a = a^2$?
4. Do you know that $-(-3) = 3$?

If three were a confident yes, read on.
:::

## Touch

Take a sheet of paper. Draw a rectangle whose sides have unknown
lengths $a$ and $b$. Now extend each side by another unknown length —
$c$ to the right of $a$ and $d$ above $b$. The big rectangle now has
sides $(a+c)$ and $(b+d)$.

The slider below shows the same picture for chosen numerical values.
Watch how the big rectangle is built from four smaller pieces.

```{ojs}
viewof aa = Inputs.range([1, 8], {value: 4, step: 1, label: "a"})
viewof bb = Inputs.range([1, 6], {value: 3, step: 1, label: "b"})
viewof cc = Inputs.range([1, 6], {value: 2, step: 1, label: "c"})
viewof dd = Inputs.range([1, 5], {value: 2, step: 1, label: "d"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const a=aa, b=bb, c=cc, d=dd;
  const k = 28;
  const W = (a+c)*k, H = (b+d)*k;
  const ab = a*b, ad=a*d, cb=c*b, cd=c*d;
  return html`<div style="font-family:system-ui;">
    <svg width="${W+40}" height="${H+30}">
      <rect x="20" y="0" width="${a*k}" height="${b*k}" fill="#bde0fe" stroke="#1d4ed8"/>
      <rect x="${20+a*k}" y="0" width="${c*k}" height="${b*k}" fill="#fed7aa" stroke="#c2410c"/>
      <rect x="20" y="${b*k}" width="${a*k}" height="${d*k}" fill="#bbf7d0" stroke="#15803d"/>
      <rect x="${20+a*k}" y="${b*k}" width="${c*k}" height="${d*k}" fill="#fecaca" stroke="#b91c1c"/>
      <text x="${20+a*k/2}" y="${b*k/2+5}" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12">a&times;b=${ab}</text>
      <text x="${20+a*k+c*k/2}" y="${b*k/2+5}" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12">c&times;b=${cb}</text>
      <text x="${20+a*k/2}" y="${b*k+d*k/2+5}" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12">a&times;d=${ad}</text>
      <text x="${20+a*k+c*k/2}" y="${b*k+d*k/2+5}" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12">c&times;d=${cd}</text>
    </svg>
    <div style="color:#444;margin-top:0.4rem;">
      Total area: (${a}+${c}) &times; (${b}+${d}) = ${a+c}&times;${b+d} = ${(a+c)*(b+d)}.
      &nbsp; Pieces: ${ab}+${cb}+${ad}+${cd} = ${ab+cb+ad+cd}.
    </div>
  </div>`;
}
```

The big area equals the sum of the four little areas, *whatever* the
side lengths. That algebraic fact is the engine of this chapter.

## Wonder

Sit with these.

1. The big rectangle has side $(a+c) \times (b+d)$. Pull out the four
   tiles. Why do you get four pieces, not three or five?
2. What if $c = 0$? What does the picture become?
3. What if $a$ and $b$ are equal, and $c$ and $d$ are also equal? What
   is the big shape?
4. Could the same picture be drawn with $a$, $c$ as letters (not
   numbers)? What would change?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## What you might notice (don't peek until you've thought)

The four tiles always exist: $ab, ad, cb, cd$. Adding them gives
$(a+c)(b+d)$. That is the **distributive law**, generalised. Three
shortcut versions of this picture — when the two sides are the same
sum, the same difference, or one of each — are the **identities** named
in this chapter.
:::

## Discover

### Problem 1 — Combine like terms

Simplify $4x + 7 - 2x + 3 - x$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Group $x$-terms together and constants together.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$x$-terms: $4x - 2x - x = x$. Constants: $7 + 3 = 10$. Answer: $x + 10$.
</details>

### Problem 2 — Multiply a monomial by a polynomial

Compute $3x \cdot (2x^2 + 5x - 4)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Distribute $3x$ to each term.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$3x \cdot 2x^2 + 3x \cdot 5x + 3x \cdot (-4) = 6x^3 + 15x^2 - 12x$.
</details>

### Problem 3 — Two binomials

Use the rectangle picture (or the FOIL pattern) to compute
$(x + 3)(x + 5)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Four tiles: $x \cdot x$, $x \cdot 5$, $3 \cdot x$, $3 \cdot 5$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$(x+3)(x+5) = x^2 + 5x + 3x + 15 = x^2 + 8x + 15$.

(Note the middle coefficient $8 = 3 + 5$. The constant $15 = 3 \cdot 5$.
That is the FOIL pattern.)
</details>

### Problem 4 — Square of a sum

Compute $(a + b)^2$ by expansion.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$(a+b)^2 = (a+b)(a+b)$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
Use FOIL.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$(a+b)(a+b) = a^2 + ab + ba + b^2 = a^2 + 2ab + b^2$.

(Picture: a square of side $a+b$ split into one $a^2$ tile, two $ab$
tiles, one $b^2$ tile. The famous *square of a sum* identity.)
</details>

### Problem 5 — Square of a difference

Compute $(a - b)^2$ similarly.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$(a-b)(a-b)$. Take care with signs.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$(a-b)(a-b) = a^2 - ab - ba + b^2 = a^2 - 2ab + b^2$.
</details>

### Problem 6 — Product of sum and difference

Compute $(a + b)(a - b)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Use FOIL. The two cross terms cancel.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$(a+b)(a-b) = a^2 - ab + ba - b^2 = a^2 - b^2$.

(Beautiful and useful — the **difference of squares** identity.)
</details>

### Problem 7 — Use one in the wild

Compute $103 \times 97$ in your head, using one of the identities you
just discovered.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$103 = 100 + 3$, $97 = 100 - 3$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$103 \times 97 = (100+3)(100-3) = 100^2 - 3^2 = 10000 - 9 = 9991$.
</details>

You have just proved (and used) the three most important identities of
school algebra:
$$(a+b)^2 = a^2 + 2ab + b^2,$$
$$(a-b)^2 = a^2 - 2ab + b^2,$$
$$(a+b)(a-b) = a^2 - b^2.$$

::: {.fun-fact}
Letters for unknowns are surprisingly recent. The French lawyer-mathematician
**François Viète** (1591, *In Artem Analyticem Isagoge*) was the first to use
*letters* — vowels for unknowns, consonants for knowns — instead of writing
out *res* ("the thing") in Latin. Half a century later, **René Descartes**
(*La Géométrie*, 1637) settled on the convention we still use today: $x, y,
z$ for unknowns and $a, b, c$ for knowns. Every identity in this chapter is
written in Descartes' notation, less than four hundred years old.
:::

::: {.key-insight}
A standard identity is a **rectangle picture in disguise**. The square of a
sum is one square plus two off-diagonal tiles plus another square; the
difference of squares is a rectangle with one corner removed and pasted on
the other side. If you can draw the rectangle, the algebra writes itself.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

An **algebraic expression** is a combination of variables and constants
joined by $+, -, \times, \div$ and powers. A **term** is a single
addend; **like terms** are terms differing only in their numerical
coefficient.

The three **standard identities**:
$$(a+b)^2 = a^2 + 2ab + b^2$$
$$(a-b)^2 = a^2 - 2ab + b^2$$
$$(a+b)(a-b) = a^2 - b^2$$

A fourth, often useful:
$$(x+a)(x+b) = x^2 + (a+b)x + ab.$$

These are *equalities for all values of the variables* — not equations
to solve.

:::

A short verse, in the spirit of Bhāskara's *Līlāvatī*:

> *Square the sum: each piece, then twice the cross.*
> *Square the diff: same shape, the cross sign flips.*
> *Sum times diff: the squares fall apart, the crosses cancel.*
> *FOIL all four — first, outer, inner, last.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> Use a standard identity to compute $(2x + 3)^2$ and then evaluate
> when $x = 5$.

Competent solver's path:

1. Identity $(a+b)^2 = a^2 + 2ab + b^2$ with $a = 2x$, $b = 3$.
2. $(2x+3)^2 = (2x)^2 + 2 \cdot 2x \cdot 3 + 3^2 = 4x^2 + 12x + 9$.
3. At $x = 5$: $4 \cdot 25 + 12 \cdot 5 + 9 = 100 + 60 + 9 = 169$.

(Double-check: $2 \cdot 5 + 3 = 13$, and $13^2 = 169$. ✓)

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** Simplify $5a + 3b - 2a + b$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$3a + 4b$.
</details>

**W2.** Expand $(x - 7)(x + 7)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Difference-of-squares identity.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$x^2 - 49$.
</details>

**W3.** Expand $(p + 2)^2$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$p^2 + 4p + 4$.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** Compute $(3x - 4y)^2$ using the appropriate identity.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$(a-b)^2 = a^2 - 2ab + b^2$ with $a=3x$, $b=4y$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$(3x)^2 - 2(3x)(4y) + (4y)^2 = 9x^2 - 24xy + 16y^2$.
</details>

**P2.** Use $(x+a)(x+b) = x^2 + (a+b)x + ab$ to expand $(x+5)(x-3)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$a = 5, b = -3$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$(x+5)(x-3) = x^2 + (5-3)x + 5 \cdot (-3) = x^2 + 2x - 15$.
</details>

**P3.** Use the difference-of-squares identity to compute $51 \times
49$ mentally.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$51 = 50+1$, $49 = 50-1$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$51 \times 49 = (50+1)(50-1) = 2500 - 1 = 2499$.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** Show that for every real $a, b$:
$(a+b)^2 - (a-b)^2 = 4ab$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Expand both squares using the identities.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$(a+b)^2 = a^2 + 2ab + b^2$, $(a-b)^2 = a^2 - 2ab + b^2$.
Subtract: $(a^2 + 2ab + b^2) - (a^2 - 2ab + b^2) = 4ab$. ✓
</details>

**S2.** If $a + b = 7$ and $ab = 12$, find $a^2 + b^2$ without solving
for $a$ and $b$ individually.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$(a+b)^2 = a^2 + 2ab + b^2$. Rearrange to solve for $a^2 + b^2$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$a^2 + b^2 = (a+b)^2 - 2ab = 49 - 24 = 25$.
</details>

### Board-Pattern Practice

**1-mark (objective).** $(x + 4)(x - 4) = ?$
(a) $x^2 - 16$ (b) $x^2 + 16$ (c) $x^2 - 8x + 16$ (d) $x^2 + 8x - 16$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Difference of squares: $x^2 - 16$. **Answer: (a).**
</details>

**2-mark (short answer).** Find the product $(2x + 3y)(2x - 3y)$ and
state the identity used.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$(a+b)(a-b) = a^2 - b^2$ with $a = 2x$, $b = 3y$.
$(2x)^2 - (3y)^2 = 4x^2 - 9y^2$.
</details>

**3-mark (short answer II).** Use a suitable identity to compute
$(99)^2$ without long multiplication.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$99 = 100 - 1$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$(100 - 1)^2 = 100^2 - 2 \cdot 100 \cdot 1 + 1 = 10000 - 200 + 1 = 9801$.
</details>

**4-mark (long answer).** (i) Expand $(x + 2y - 3z)^2$. (ii) If $x + 2y
- 3z = 7$ and $x^2 + 4y^2 + 9z^2 = 49$, find the value of
$2xy - 6yz - 3xz$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
For three terms, use $(a+b+c)^2 = a^2 + b^2 + c^2 + 2(ab + bc + ca)$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>

**(i)** Let $a = x$, $b = 2y$, $c = -3z$.
$(x + 2y - 3z)^2 = x^2 + 4y^2 + 9z^2 + 2(2xy + 2y(-3z) + x(-3z))$
$= x^2 + 4y^2 + 9z^2 + 4xy - 12yz - 6xz$.

**(ii)** Substitute the given values into the expansion:
$49 = 49 + 2(2xy - 6yz - 3xz)$
$0 = 2(2xy - 6yz - 3xz)$
$\boxed{2xy - 6yz - 3xz = 0}$.
</details>

::: {.callout-note}
**NCERT Exemplar pointer:** Exemplar Problem 8.4 (using $(a+b+c)^2$ to
extract a cross-term sum) is a near-twin of the 4-mark item.
**Previous-year pointer:** CBSE 2019 Set 1, Q19 ("compute $103^2$
using an identity") matches the 3-mark style.
:::

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"$(a+b)^2 = a^2 + b^2$."** Almost always wrong. The cross term
   $2ab$ is missing. Picture the rectangle: it has *two* off-diagonal
   tiles, not zero.
2. **"$(a-b)^2$ is negative."** No — it's always $\geq 0$. The square
   of a real number is non-negative.
3. **"FOIL only works on $(a+b)(c+d)$."** It works on any two
   binomials. The four pieces are *first*, *outer*, *inner*, *last*.
   Identities are just FOIL with patterns.
:::

### Takeaways

If you remember nothing else from this chapter, remember these:

1. An [algebraic expression](/glossary.qmd#algebraic-expression) is just
   numbers and letters combined by the four operations and powers — and
   *like terms* live in the same column when you tidy up.
2. The three standard identities are the **rectangle picture** told
   algebraically: $(a+b)^2$, $(a-b)^2$, and the
   [difference of squares](/glossary.qmd#difference-of-squares).
3. Identities are *equalities for every value* — they let you transform
   an expression's shape without changing what it is. That move is the
   whole game of algebra.

### Self-Assessment

Be honest with yourself.

1. Could you re-derive $(a+b)^2 = a^2 + 2ab + b^2$ from a rectangle
   picture, on a blank sheet, without notes?
2. Did you solve at least 6 of the 7 *Discover* problems and 5 of the 7
   *Practice* problems without peeking past Hint 1?
3. Given a number puzzle like $103 \times 97$, can you spot which
   identity to deploy?

- **3 yes:** move on to **[Mensuration](../09-mensuration/index.qmd)**.
- **2 yes:** redo *Stretch S2* and the 4-mark board item, then move on.
- **0–1 yes:** rebuild from *Discover Problems 4–6*. The three
  identities are the foundation of the *next* chapter.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[Intro to Variables](../../tier-1-foundations/11-intro-variables/index.qmd)** — letters as placeholders.
- **[Multiplication as Area](../../tier-1-foundations/05-multiply-as-area/index.qmd)** — the rectangle picture that proves the identities.

### What this unlocks

- **[Factorisation](../12-factorisation/index.qmd)** — running the identities *backward*.
- **[Class 9 — Polynomials](../../tier-cbse-09-class-9/02-polynomials/index.qmd)** — adds the Remainder and Factor theorems.
- **[Class 10 — Polynomials](../../tier-cbse-10-class-10/02-polynomials/index.qmd)** — relations between roots and coefficients.

### NCERT chapter alignment

- **Class 8, Chapter 8 — Algebraic Expressions and Identities** (NCERT
  2023 rationalised syllabus). Covers Sections 8.1–8.5: terms, like
  terms, multiplication of monomials and polynomials, the standard
  identities.
- **Sister synthesis lesson** (different pedagogical angle):
  [`t2.factoring-polynomials`](../../tier-2-prealgebra/12-factoring-polynomials/index.qmd)
  inverts these identities; this chapter establishes them by direct
  expansion.
- **Read alongside the NCERT textbook**, which remains the canonical
  source for the board paper.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Research.** The identity $a^2 - b^2 = (a+b)(a-b)$ is the seed of
factorisation in number theory; Fermat's factorisation method (used by
hand to break small RSA-like keys) starts from this rectangle picture.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / ML.** The bias-variance decomposition in machine learning is a
direct application of $(a+b)^2 = a^2 + 2ab + b^2$ applied to the
expected-error term — the same algebra you learned today, used by
practitioners every day.
:::
